title: Bacteriophage engineering via semi-synthesis
abstract: The present disclosure provides methods of generating recombinant bacteriophage genomes via semi-synthesis. Specifically, the present technology provides methods of integrating a heterologous nucleic acid sequence into a bacteriophage genome, and isolating recombinant bacteriophages that express the heterologous nucleic acid sequence.
claims:
Claims 1. A method for generating a recombinant bacteriophage genome comprising: (a) generating a plurality of amplicons from a template comprising a first bacteriophage DNA genome, wherein the plurality of amplicons collectively span the entire length of the first bacteriophage DNA genome, wherein at least one end of each amplicon comprises a sequence that is homologous to an opposite end of another amplicon and wherein each amplicon is no more than 15 kilobases in length; and (b) recombining in vitro the plurality of amplicons with a heterologous nucleic acid in the presence of a recombination system under conditions to produce a recombinant bacteriophage genome. 2. The method of claim 1, wherein the heterologous nucleic acid comprises a 3' flanking region that is homologous to the 5' end of an amplicon. 3. The method of claim 1 or 2, wherein the heterologous nucleic acid comprises a 5' flanking region that is homologous to the 3' end of an amplicon. 4. The method of any one of claims 1-3, further comprising propagating the recombinant bacteriophage genome in a non-natural or natural bacterial host. 5. The method of any one of claims 1-4, wherein the first bacteriophage DNA genome corresponds to a bacteriophage family or order selected from the group consisting of Myoviridae, Styloviridae, Siphoviridae, Pedoviridae, Tectiviridae, Leviviridae, Podoviridae, and Plasmaviridae . 6. The method of any one of claims 1-5, wherein the first bacteriophage DNA genome is derived from a bacteriophage genus selected from the group consisting of T7-like phage, phiKMV-like phage, LUZ24-like phage, phiKZ-like phage, PBl-like phage, Felix-Ol-like phage, T4-like phage, phi92-like phage, rV5-like phage, SP6-like phage, N4-like phage, phiEco32-like phage, T5-like phage, KP34-like phage, KP15-like phage, GAP227-like phage, AP22-like phage, phiFel-like phage, Sap6-like phage, Silvia-like phage, Kay-like phage, Twort-like phage, P68-like phage, and phiETA-like phage. 7. The method of any one of claims 1-6, wherein the first bacteriophage DNA genome corresponds to Klebsiella phage Kl 1, lambda phage, Enterobacteria phage T2, Enterobacteria phage Tl, Enterobacteria phage T7, Enterobacteria phage T5, Enterobacteria phage PI, Enterobacteria phage PRD1, K1E phage, Kl-5 phage, RB49 

 phage, RBI 6 phage, KP15 phage, KP27 phage, Miro phage, Matisse phage, phiEap-3 phage, ECP3 phage, EFDG1 phage, EFLK1 phage, vB_Efae230P-4 phage, vB_EfaP_FME195 phage, SA11 phage, Stau2 phage, K phage, Gl phage, SA12 phage, 812 phage, P68 phage, SAP-2 phage, 44AHJD phage, or SA97 phage. 8. The method of any one of claims 1-7, wherein the recombination system comprises a 5 '-3' exonuclease, a DNA polymerase, and a DNA ligase. 9. The method of claim 8, wherein the 5'-3' exonuclease is T5 exonuclease, the DNA polymerase is Phusion Â® DNA polymerase, and the DNA ligase is Taq ligase. 10. The method of any one of claims 1-7, wherein the recombination system comprises a 3 '-5' exonuclease, a DNA polymerase, and a DNA ligase. 11. The method of any one of claims 1-10, wherein the heterologous nucleic acid comprises an open reading frame that encodes a bioluminescent protein, a fluorescent protein, a chemiluminescent protein, a phage protein that modifies host range, or any combination thereof. 12. The method of claim 11, wherein the open reading frame of the heterologous nucleic acid is operably linked to an expression control sequence that is capable of directing expression of the bioluminescent protein, the fluorescent protein, the chemiluminescent protein, the phage protein that modifies host range, or any combination thereof. 13. The method of claim 11 or 12, wherein the bioluminescent protein is Aequorin, firefly luciferase, Renilla luciferase, red luciferase, luxAB, or nanoluciferase. 14. The method of claim 11 or 12, wherein the chemiluminescent protein is Î²- galactosidase, horseradish peroxidase (HRP), or alkaline phosphatase. 15. The method of claim 11 or 12, wherein the fluorescent protein is TagBFP, Azurite, EBFP2, mKalamal, Sirius, Sapphire, T-Sapphire, ECFP, Cerulean, SCFP3A, mTurquoise, monomeric Midoriishi-Cyan, TagCFP, mTFPl, GFP, EGFP, Emerald, Superfolder GFP, Monomeric Azami Green, TagGFP2, mUKG, mWasabi, EYFP, Citrine, Venus, SYFP2, TagYFP, Monomeric Kusabira-Orange, ÏÎ¹ÎÎÎº, mK02, mOrange, mOrange2, mRaspberry, mCherry, dsRed, mStrawberry, mTangerine, tdTomato, TagRFP, TagRFP-T, mApple, mRuby, mPlum, HcRed-Tandem, mKate2, mNeptune, NirFP, TagRFP657, IFP1.4, iRFP, mKeima Red, LSS-mKatel, LSS-mKate2, PA-GFP, PAmCherry 1, PATagRFP, Kaede 

 (green), Kaede (red), KikGRl (green), KikGRl (red), PS-CFP2, PS-CFP2, mEos2 (green), mEos2 (red), PSmOrange, or Dronpa. 16. The method of claim 11 or 12, wherein the phage protein that modifies host range is a tail spike protein, a structural phage virion protein involved with bacterial cell attachment, or a structural phage virion protein involved with degradation of bacterial cell wall components. 17. The method of claim 16, wherein the tail spike protein is gpl 1, gpl2, or gpl7. 18. The method of any one of claims 12-17, wherein the expression control sequence is an inducible promoter or a constitutive promoter. 19. The method of any one of claims 1-18, wherein the heterologous nucleic acid is about 100-500 base pairs in length. 20. The method of any one of claims 1-18, wherein the heterologous nucleic acid is about 500-1500 base pairs in length. 21. The method of claim 7, wherein the recombinant bacteriophage genome is a recombinant Klebsiella phage Kl 1 comprising the nucleic acid sequence of SEQ ID NO: 1. 22. The method of claim 7, wherein the recombinant bacteriophage genome is a recombinant Enterobacteria phage T7 comprising the nucleic acid sequence of SEQ ID NO: 2. 23. The method of claim 21, wherein the plurality of amplicons were generated using one or more primer pairs selected from the group consisting of SEQ ID NO: 3 and SEQ ID NO: 4; SEQ ID NO: 5 and SEQ ID NO: 6; SEQ ID NO: 7 and SEQ ID NO: 8; SEQ ID NO: 9 and SEQ ID NO: 10; and SEQ ID NO: 11 and SEQ ID NO: 12. 24. The method of claim 22, wherein the plurality of amplicons were generated using one or more primer pairs selected from the group consisting of SEQ ID NO: 15 and SEQ ID NO: 16; SEQ ID NO: 17 and SEQ ID NO: 18; SEQ ID NO: 19 and SEQ ID NO: 20; and SEQ ID NO: 21 and SEQ ID NO: 22. 25. A method for generating a semi-synthetic recombinant bacteriophage genome from a bacteriophage DNA template comprising a first genomic region and a second genomic region comprising: (a) generating a first plurality of amplicons, wherein the first plurality of amplicons collectively span the entire length of the first genomic region of the bacteriophage DNA 

 template, wherein at least one end of each amplicon of the first plurality of amplicons comprises a sequence that is homologous to an opposite end of another amplicon of the first plurality of amplicons and wherein each amplicon of the first plurality of amplicons is no more than 15 kilobases in length; (b) recombining the first plurality of amplicons and a heterologous nucleic acid in vitro in the presence of a recombination system under conditions to produce a first recombinant bacteriophage genomic fragment; and (c) introducing the first recombinant bacteriophage genomic fragment into a first expression vector to produce a first circular phage expression vector. 26. The method of claim 25, further comprising (a) generating a second plurality of amplicons, wherein the second plurality of amplicons collectively span the entire length of the second genomic region of the bacteriophage DNA template, wherein at least one end of each amplicon of the second plurality of amplicons comprises a sequence that is homologous to an opposite end of another amplicon of the second plurality of amplicons and wherein each amplicon of the second plurality of amplicons is no more than 15 kilobases in length; (b) recombining the second plurality of amplicons in vitro in the presence of a recombination system under conditions to produce a second bacteriophage genomic fragment; and (c) introducing the second bacteriophage genomic fragment into a second expression vector to produce a second circular phage expression vector. 27. The method of claim 26, wherein the first circular phage expression vector comprises a first unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence that is located 3' to the first recombinant bacteriophage genomic fragment and the second circular phage expression vector comprises a second unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence that is located 5' to the second bacteriophage genomic fragment. 28. The method of claim 26, wherein the first circular phage expression vector comprises a first unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence that is located 5' to the first recombinant bacteriophage genomic fragment and the second circular phage expression vector comprises a second unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence that is located 3' to the second bacteriophage genomic fragment. 29. The method of claim 27 or 28, comprising cleaving the first circular phage expression vector with a first restriction enzyme that recognizes the first unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence to produce a first linear phage expression vector. 30. The method of claim 29, wherein the first restriction enzyme cleaves within the first unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence or at a position near the first unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence. 31. The method of any one of claims 27-30, comprising cleaving the second circular phage expression vector with a second restriction enzyme that recognizes the second unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence to produce a second linear phage expression vector. 32. The method of claim 31, wherein the second restriction enzyme cleaves within the second unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence or at a position near the second unique restriction enzyme recognition sequence. 33. The method of any one of claims 29-32, further comprising transforming a non- natural or natural bacterial host cell with the first linear phage expression vector and/or the second linear phage expression vector. 34. The method of claim 25 or claim 26, further comprising transforming a non-natural or natural bacterial host cell with the first circular phage expression vector and/or the second circular phage expression vector. 35. The method of claim 33 or 34, wherein the non-natural or natural bacterial host cell comprises a non-endogenous inducible recombination system. 36. The method of claim 35, wherein the non-endogenous inducible recombination system comprises lambda Red proteins Gam, Exo, and Beta operably linked to an inducible promoter. 37. The method of claim 36, wherein the inducible promoter is araB. 38. The method of claim 37, wherein the non-endogenous inducible recombination system is induced by the addition of arabinose. 39. The method of any one of claims 25-38, wherein the first genomic region has a length of 75,000 bases- 150,000 bases and the second genomic region has a length of 75,000 bases- 150,000 bases. 40. A method for generating a plurality of semi-synthetic recombinant bacteriophage genomes comprising: (a) generating a plurality of amplicons from a template comprising a first bacteriophage DNA genome, wherein the plurality of amplicons collectively span the entire length of the first bacteriophage DNA genome, wherein at least one end of each amplicon comprises a sequence that is homologous to an opposite end of another amplicon and wherein each amplicon is no more than 15 kilobases in length; (b) recombining in vitro the plurality of amplicons with a heterologous nucleic acid in the presence of a recombination system under conditions to produce a recombinant linear bacteriophage genome; (c) recombining in vitro the recombinant linear bacteriophage genome with a DNA bridge in the presence of a recombination system under conditions to produce a recombinant circular bacteriophage genome; and (d) amplifying the recombinant circular bacteriophage genome using rolling circle amplification to generate a plurality of semi -synthetic recombinant bacteriophage genomes. 41. The method of claim 40, wherein the heterologous nucleic acid comprises a 3' flanking region that is homologous to the 5' end of an amplicon. 42. The method of claim 40 or 41, wherein the heterologous nucleic acid comprises a 5' flanking region that is homologous to the 3' end of an amplicon. 43. The method of any one of claims 40-42, wherein the DNA bridge comprises a 3' flanking region that is homologous to the 5' end of the recombinant linear bacteriophage genome. 44. The method of any one of claims 40-43, wherein the DNA bridge comprises a 5' flanking region that is homologous to the 3' end of the recombinant linear bacteriophage genome. 45. The method of any one of claims 40-44, wherein the length of the DNA bridge is at least 50 base pairs. 46. The method of any one of claims 40-45, further comprising propagating the plurality of semi-synthetic recombinant bacteriophage genomes in a non-natural or natural bacterial host cell. 47. The method of claim 46, wherein the non-natural or natural bacterial host cell comprises a non-endogenous inducible recombination system. 48. The method of claim 47, wherein the non-endogenous inducible recombination system comprises lambda Red proteins Gam, Exo, and Beta operably linked to an inducible promoter. 49. The method of claim 48, wherein the inducible promoter is araB. 50. The method of claim 49, wherein the non-endogenous inducible recombination system is induced by the addition of arabinose.
